subject: Pulmonology
question: A 68-year-old man with 40 pack-year smoking history presents with progressive dyspnea and chronic productive cough over 10 years. PFT shows FEV1/FVC <0.70 with FEV1 45% predicted. No significant bronchodilator reversibility. What is the most appropriate classification and treatment?

options.text: GOLD Stage 2 (Moderate) - LABA or LAMA monotherapy
options.explanation: FEV1 45% = GOLD Stage 3 (Severe), not Stage 2 (50-79%). Stage 2 uses monotherapy. Stage 3 requires dual bronchodilation.
GOLD Stage 3 = FEV1 30-49%. Treatment: dual bronchodilation with LABA + LAMA. Add ICS if recurrent exacerbations or eosinophil count >300. Smoking cessation is most important intervention.

options.text: GOLD Stage 4 (Very Severe) - LABA + LAMA + ICS
options.explanation: GOLD Stage 4 = FEV1 <30% or FEV1 <50% with chronic respiratory failure. This patient is Stage 3 (FEV1 45%). ICS added for recurrent exacerbations, not based on FEV1 alone.
Oral steroids are for ACUTE exacerbations (5-7 day course), not chronic management of stable COPD. Long-term oral steroids cause osteoporosis, diabetes, myopathy. Inhaled therapies preferred.
Theophylline is a third-line option due to narrow therapeutic window and side effects (nausea, arrhythmias, seizures). Not recommended as monotherapy. Bronchodilators (LABA/LAMA) are first-line.

educationalObjective: Classify COPD severity using GOLD criteria (based on FEV1%) and select appropriate pharmacotherapy: dual bronchodilation for severe disease.
keyTakeaway: GOLD Stage 3 (FEV1 30-49%): LABA + LAMA dual bronchodilation. Add ICS for recurrent exacerbations. Smoking cessation crucial.
